question: My employer has not paid my holiday pay. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid you for holiday days you have taken, you may be entitled to receive holiday pay. Holiday pay is the payment you receive for time off work that you are legally entitled to. You should first check your employment contract and any company policies to confirm your entitlement to holiday pay. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, you should raise the issue with your employer and ask them to pay what they owe you.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. You may also seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice for help with your situation. If necessary, you might be able to take legal action through an employment tribunal for unpaid holiday pay.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer and explain the issue, providing any evidence you have.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the problem is not resolved informally.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.  
Step 5: Consider legal action through an employment tribunal if all else fails.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: How is holiday pay calculated if I work irregular hours?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your average pay over a relevant period, usually the last 52 weeks in which you were paid. This means your employer will look at how much you earned and how many hours you worked during that time to work out an average weekly pay. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at your ‘normal’ rate of pay, which includes things like commission and regular overtime payments, but usually not bonuses.

Your employer cannot include holiday pay within your hourly rate (known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’) if your leave year began after 31 March 2024. If your leave year started on or before that date, different rules may apply. If you have questions about your specific situation, you can contact Acas for guidance.

Step 1: Your employer calculates your average weekly pay based on your pay and hours worked over the last 52 weeks (or fewer if you have not worked that long).  
Step 2: Your holiday pay is then paid at this average ‘normal’ rate for each week of statutory leave you take.  
Step 3: If you are unsure how your holiday pay has been calculated, ask your employer for a clear explanation or contact Acas for advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Can my employer include holiday pay in my hourly rate?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers generally cannot include an amount for holiday pay in your hourly rate, a practice known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’. However, there is an exception for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers: for these workers, rolled-up holiday pay can be used unless their leave year began on or before 31 March 2024. This means that for most workers, holiday pay should be paid separately from their hourly wage.

If you work irregular hours or part-year, your holiday pay is usually calculated based on the hours you have worked, rather than being included in your hourly rate. For more detailed guidance, you can use the holiday entitlement calculator or contact Acas for advice.

Step 1: Check if you are classified as a worker with irregular hours or part-year work.  
Step 2: Determine when your leave year started (before or after 31 March 2024).  
Step 3: If you are not eligible for rolled-up holiday pay, your holiday pay should be paid separately from your hourly rate.  
Step 4: Contact Acas or use the holiday entitlement calculator for help calculating your holiday pay.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: I have taken annual leave but did not get paid for it. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you have taken annual leave but were not paid for it, you are generally entitled to holiday pay for the time you took off. Employers must pay you for statutory holiday entitlement, which is a legal right. If you believe you should have been paid for your holiday but were not, you should first check your employment contract and company policies to confirm your entitlement. If your employer refuses to pay, you can gather evidence such as your contract, payslips, and any communication about your holiday to support your claim.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any company policies regarding holiday pay.  
Step 2: Collect evidence showing you took the holiday and were not paid (e.g., holiday request forms, emails, payslips).  
Step 3: Raise the issue with your employer or payroll department, providing your evidence and asking for the unpaid holiday pay.  
Step 4: If your employer still does not pay, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: How much holiday am I legally entitled to in the UK?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers are legally entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year. This entitlement applies whether you work full-time, part-time, or under a zero-hours contract. The 5.6 weeks can include bank holidays, but this depends on your employment contract. For example, if you work five days a week, this equates to 28 days of paid holiday annually. Part-time workers receive holiday entitlement proportional to the hours they work, ensuring they are not treated less favourably than full-time workers.

If you have been employed for less than a year, you are entitled to a proportion of the full year's holiday based on how long you have worked. Holiday entitlement starts accruing from your first day of work and continues during periods such as probation, sick leave, and statutory parental leave. Some employers may offer more than the statutory minimum, known as enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement, which will be detailed in your contract.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or written statement of employment particulars to see your holiday entitlement and whether bank holidays are included.  
Step 2: If unsure, speak with your manager, HR department, or employer for clarification.  
Step 3: Use the holiday calculator on the GOV.UK website to estimate your entitlement based on your working pattern.  
Step 4: Contact the Acas helpline if you have further questions about your holiday entitlement.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Does holiday continue to build up while I am off sick?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, holiday entitlement continues to build up while you are off work sick. This means that your right to paid annual leave is maintained during periods of sickness absence, including long-term sickness. Employers must allow you to accumulate holiday entitlement during these times, just as you would if you were working.

If you have questions about how your holiday entitlement is calculated or how to take your holiday after being off sick, you can speak to your employer or contact the Acas helpline for guidance.

Step 1: Confirm your holiday entitlement with your employer or HR department.  
Step 2: Keep records of your sickness absence and holiday requests.  
Step 3: Contact Acas if you need further clarification or support.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What happens to my unused holiday when I leave my job?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave your job, you are entitled to be paid for any unused statutory holiday entitlement that you have accrued but not taken. This payment is usually included in your final pay. Your employer should calculate how much holiday pay you are owed based on your usual pay and the amount of holiday you have left. The final pay may also include other elements like notice pay or redundancy pay, but the holiday pay should be clearly identified on your payslip.

If you think your final pay does not include the correct amount for unused holiday, you should first speak to your employer to clarify how it was calculated. Your final pay should be paid on your normal payday, even if you leave partway through a pay period. If you do not receive a payslip or have questions about your final pay, you can contact the Acas helpline for guidance.

Step 1: Check your final payslip to see if unused holiday pay is included and clearly identified.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for an explanation if you think the amount is incorrect.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice if you cannot resolve the issue with your employer.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Can my employer refuse my holiday request?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can refuse your holiday request, but they must have a good reason for doing so. While workers have the right to take paid annual leave, employers can refuse requests if it would disrupt the business or if there are other valid operational reasons. However, employers should consider your request fairly and consistently.

If your holiday request is refused, you can try to discuss alternative dates with your employer. If you believe your holiday rights or pay are not being met, there are ways to resolve the dispute, including seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Make your holiday request in writing, giving reasonable notice.  
Step 2: If refused, ask your employer for the reason and discuss alternative dates.  
Step 3: If you feel your rights are not respected, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer force me to take holiday?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers can require you to take holiday at certain times, but they must give you notice that is at least twice as long as the amount of holiday they want you to take. For example, if your employer wants you to take one week of holiday, they must give you at least two weeks' notice. This is to ensure you have enough time to plan and prepare.

Holiday entitlement is a legal right, and employers must provide it. You also have the right to holiday pay during your leave. If you think your employer is not following the rules about holiday entitlement or forcing you to take holiday unfairly, you can raise the issue with them or seek advice from organisations like ACAS.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and company holiday policy for any specific rules about taking holiday.  
Step 2: If your employer wants you to take holiday, ensure they give you the correct notice period (twice the length of the holiday).  
Step 3: If you believe your rights are not being respected, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Do part-time workers get holiday pay?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, part-time workers in the UK are entitled to holiday pay. By law, all workers, including part-time employees, have the right to 5.6 weeks of statutory paid holiday per year. The amount of holiday pay a part-time worker receives is proportional to the hours they work, ensuring they are not treated less favourably than full-time workers. If an employer offers more than the statutory minimum holiday entitlement to full-time workers, they must also provide part-time workers with a proportionate increase.

Holiday entitlement starts accruing from the first day of work and includes periods such as probation, sick leave, and statutory parental leave. Part-time workers should check their contracts for any enhanced holiday entitlements beyond the statutory minimum.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for details on holiday entitlement and pay.  
Step 2: Calculate your holiday entitlement based on the hours you work, using the 5.6 weeks statutory minimum as a baseline.  
Step 3: If unsure or if you believe your rights are not being met, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer pay me instead of giving me holiday?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers have a legal right to paid annual leave, which means employers must provide holiday entitlement and holiday pay. Generally, employers should allow you to take your holiday rather than just paying you for it. However, there are some exceptions. For example, if you leave your job and have unused holiday entitlement, your employer must pay you for that untaken holiday in your final pay.

Employers cannot include holiday pay as part of your hourly rate (known as "rolled-up holiday pay") for most workers, except in certain cases like irregular-hours or part-year workers, and only if the leave year began before 31 March 2024. If you think your employer is not following the rules about holiday or holiday pay, you can contact ACAS for advice.

Step 1: Check your contract and holiday entitlement to understand your rights.  
Step 2: If you believe your employer is not giving you your holiday or paying you correctly, raise the issue with them.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you can contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for further help.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Is rolled-up holiday pay legal in the UK?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Rolled-up holiday pay, where an employer includes an amount for holiday pay in the hourly rate, is generally not allowed in the UK for most workers. However, there is an exception for irregular-hours and part-year workers, where rolled-up holiday pay can be used unless their leave year began on or before 31 March 2024. This means that for most workers, holiday pay must be paid separately and not included in their hourly wage.

If you have questions about how holiday pay should be calculated or your specific situation, you can contact the Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration Service (Acas) for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: My employer has not paid my wages on payday. What should I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to them to find out why the payment is late. It might be a mistake they can quickly fix. If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract, to support your claim.

If your employer still does not pay you, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. It is usually best to try to resolve the issue without legal action if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to ask why you haven’t been paid.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer if possible.  
Step 3: If still unpaid, consider legal action by contacting an adviser or making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer deducted money from my wages without explaining why.

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only deduct money from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. If they have deducted money without explaining why, this may not be lawful. For certain deductions, like for damaged property or missing money from the till, your employer must have a written agreement, cannot deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and must give you written details of each deduction.

If you don’t think your employer should have deducted money, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to see if such deductions are allowed. If you are still unsure or disagree, you can seek advice from an adviser or a trade union representative if you have one.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If you disagree or don’t get a satisfactory answer, get advice from an employment adviser, trade union, or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I think I am being paid below minimum wage. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage (NMW) or National Living Wage (NLW), you should first check the current rates for your age group or apprenticeship status. Employers are legally required to pay at least the minimum wage for the hours you work. If you find your pay is below these rates, you should talk to your employer to raise the issue and show any evidence you have, such as payslips or your contract.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit for tribunal claims, usually within 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid. You can also seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for support.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age and status.  
Step 2: Gather evidence like payslips and your employment contract.  
Step 3: Talk to your employer about the issue.  
Step 4: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 5: Consider legal action if the problem is not resolved, and seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What can I do if my employer pays me late every month?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer pays you late every month, you should first talk to them to find out why your pay is delayed and explain how it affects you. It’s important to keep a record of when you were supposed to be paid and when you actually received your wages. If your employer has made a mistake, ask them to pay you as soon as possible rather than waiting until the next pay day.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If the problem continues and you are not paid what you are owed, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unauthorised deduction from wages. However, this should be a last resort, especially if you want to continue working there. Keep in mind that the time limit for starting an employment tribunal claim is usually three months minus one day from the date the payment was due.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the late payments.  
Step 2: Keep records of pay dates and amounts received.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the problem continues.  
Step 4: Consider legal action if your employer still does not pay on time, but seek advice first.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer make deductions from my wages?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only make deductions from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. Common reasons for deductions include overpayments, damaged property, missing money from the till, or the cost of a uniform. However, there are specific rules your employer must follow, especially for deductions related to missing money or damaged stock: they must have a written agreement, not deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and provide written details of each deduction.

If you think your employer has made an unlawful deduction, you should first check your contract and any agreements you have. If you disagree with the deduction, you should talk to your employer or seek advice from an adviser. If the deduction reduces your pay below the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage, your employer may be breaking the law.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to understand why the deduction was made and explain if you disagree.  
Step 3: Seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser if needed.  
Step 4: If unresolved, consider raising a formal grievance or taking legal action, such as an employment tribunal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer has not paid me for overtime. Is that legal?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, whether your employer must pay you for overtime depends on your employment contract and the terms agreed between you and your employer. There is no legal requirement for employers to pay extra for overtime unless your contract or workplace policy says so. However, your employer must pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for all hours worked, including overtime. If your total pay divided by the total hours worked (including overtime) falls below the minimum wage, your employer is not meeting their legal obligations.

If you believe you have not been paid correctly for overtime, you should first check your contract and payslips to understand what you are entitled to. Then, discuss the issue with your employer or payroll department. If this does not resolve the problem, you may consider seeking advice or making a claim for unpaid wages.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and payslips to confirm your overtime pay terms.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the unpaid overtime.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified adviser about your options for claiming unpaid wages.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What should be included on my payslip?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

A payslip should clearly show details about your pay and any deductions made. This typically includes your gross pay (the total amount before deductions), the amount of tax and National Insurance contributions taken off, any other deductions such as pension contributions or student loan repayments, and your net pay (the amount you take home). It should also include the pay period the payslip covers and your employer’s name.

Having a payslip helps you check that you are being paid correctly, including whether your employer is paying at least the minimum wage and making lawful deductions. If you think something is wrong with your payslip or pay, you should first talk to your employer to clarify the issue.

Step 1: Check your payslip for all the required information including gross pay, deductions, and net pay.  
Step 2: Compare the payslip details with your contract and hours worked.  
Step 3: If you spot any discrepancies or missing information, raise the issue with your employer or payroll department.  
Step 4: If the problem is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: Can my employer withhold my final pay when I leave?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave a job, your employer must pay you your final pay, which can be different from your usual pay because it may include things like holiday pay, deductions for training courses, or redundancy pay. Your final pay should be given on your normal payday, even if you leave partway through a pay period. Employers should clearly explain how your final pay was calculated, and this should be shown on your payslip.

Your employer cannot withhold your final pay without a lawful reason. If you do not receive your final pay or payslip, you should first speak to your employer to clarify the situation. If you still do not get paid, you may be able to take further action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court, but there are time limits for doing this.

Step 1: Check your final payslip and ask your employer to explain any deductions or changes.  
Step 2: If you do not receive your final pay or payslip, contact your employer to request it.  
Step 3: If your employer refuses to pay, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your options for making a claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What can I do if my employer owes me commission?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer owes you commission, this is considered part of your pay and you have the right to be paid what you are owed according to your employment contract. You should first try to resolve the issue by talking to your employer and showing any evidence you have, such as your contract or records of sales that should have earned commission. If your employer refuses to pay, you can raise a formal grievance with them if they have a grievance procedure.

If the problem is not resolved, you may be able to make a claim for unlawful deduction from wages through an employment tribunal. The deadline to start this process is usually 3 months minus one day from when the commission should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. It is advisable to gather all relevant evidence and consider seeking advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

Step 1: Talk to your employer and provide evidence of the commission owed.  
Step 2: Raise a formal grievance if talking does not resolve the issue.  
Step 3: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the 3-month deadline.  
Step 4: If the tribunal deadline is missed, consider a small claims court claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: Is it legal for my employer to charge me for training?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your employer can require you to do training, including mandatory training, as part of your job. However, even if your contract says you won’t be paid for training time, your employer cannot use this to avoid paying you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for the hours spent training. This means you should still receive at least the legal minimum hourly pay for any training time.

If your employer is charging you for training, this is generally not allowed, especially if it results in your pay falling below the minimum wage. You should check your contract and any company policies about training costs and pay. If you believe your employer is deducting money unlawfully or not paying you properly for training time, you may want to gather evidence such as your contract and pay slips.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any training policies.
Step 2: Check if your pay for training hours meets or exceeds the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage.
Step 3: If you think your employer is charging you unfairly or not paying minimum wage, consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: My employer says they overpaid me and wants the money back. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says they overpaid you and wants the money back, they can deduct the overpaid amount from your wages, but only if you have agreed to this in writing or if your employment contract allows it. If you don’t agree that you were overpaid, you should explain why and ask your employer to pay you what you believe you are owed. If you were overpaid but cannot afford to repay the full amount immediately, you can ask your employer if they will accept smaller deductions over a longer period.

Your employer must follow certain rules if they are deducting money for overpayments. For example, they cannot deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the overpayment, and must give you written details of each deduction on your payslip. If you think your employer has made an unlawful deduction, you should check your contract and any written agreements, and consider getting advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: If you disagree with the overpayment claim, explain your reasons to your employer.  
Step 3: If you agree but cannot repay immediately, ask if smaller repayments are possible.  
Step 4: If deductions are being made, ensure they comply with the legal rules (e.g., no more than 10% per payday).  
Step 5: Seek advice if you believe deductions are unlawful or if you need help resolving the dispute.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer change my pay without telling me?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer should not change your pay without informing you. Any changes to your pay should be agreed upon, usually in writing, as part of your contract or through a formal variation to your contract. If your employer deducts money from your wages or changes your pay, they must have a legal right to do so, such as a written agreement or a clause in your contract. If you notice a deduction or change you don’t agree with, you should check your contract and speak to your employer to understand why the change was made.

If your employer has made an unlawful deduction or changed your pay without your agreement, you can raise the issue with them first. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court, but it is usually best to seek advice before doing so.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for any clauses about pay changes or deductions.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department to ask why your pay has changed.  
Step 3: If you disagree with the change and cannot resolve it informally, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified adviser.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What should I do if I have not been paid for work I have already done?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid you for work you have already done, you should first talk to them to find out why. It’s best to speak to whoever handles payroll or your manager and explain the problem clearly, showing any evidence like payslips or your contract. If it was a mistake, ask them to pay you straight away rather than waiting until the next pay day.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If that still doesn’t work, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. You must usually start this process within 3 months minus a day from when you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim through a small claims court instead. It’s important to keep records of all communications and evidence about your pay.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the missing pay.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from a trade union representative or an adviser.  
Step 4: If necessary, start a claim at an employment tribunal within the time limit or use the small claims court.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I was dismissed without a meeting. Is that unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed without a meeting can be a concern because employers are generally expected to follow a fair and reasonable procedure before dismissing someone. This usually includes investigating the situation and giving the employee a chance to respond, often through a meeting or hearing. If your employer dismissed you without any meeting or opportunity to explain your side, this could mean they did not follow a fair procedure.

However, whether the dismissal is unfair depends on other factors too, such as whether the employer had a valid reason for dismissal and acted reasonably in the circumstances. If you have been employed for at least two years, you may have the right to challenge an unfair dismissal. You can also ask your employer for a written explanation of why you were dismissed.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for two years or more.  
Step 2: Consider whether your employer followed a fair procedure, including whether you were given a chance to respond.  
Step 3: If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: My employer dismissed me and refused to let me appeal.
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has dismissed you and refused to allow you to appeal, this could be a concern because employers are expected to follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing someone. A fair dismissal process usually includes giving you the chance to appeal the decision. If the employer does not allow an appeal, it may affect whether the dismissal is considered fair, especially if you believe the reason for dismissal was not valid or the procedure was not properly followed.

You should ask your employer for the reasons for your dismissal in writing if you have been employed for at least two years or if you are on maternity or adoption leave. If you think your dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal, but it is often best to try to resolve the issue with your employer first.

Step 1: Request a written explanation of your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Ask your employer if there is any internal appeal process you can follow.  
Step 3: If you are refused an appeal and believe the dismissal was unfair, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your options, including making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: Can I be dismissed without notice?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, you can be dismissed without notice in certain situations, such as for gross misconduct (for example, violence or theft). This is called summary dismissal. However, in most cases, your employer must give you notice either as stated in your contract or at least the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. Your employer must have a valid reason for dismissing you and act reasonably and consistently in the circumstances.

If you are dismissed, your employer should tell you why you have been dismissed, when your employment ends, and your right to appeal. If you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which your employer must provide within 14 days.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for your notice period.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for a written explanation if you are dismissed.  
Step 3: If you believe your dismissal was unfair, consider appealing or seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What is constructive dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Constructive dismissal happens when an employee resigns because their employer has behaved in a way that fundamentally breaches their employment contract. This could include things like a significant change to their job duties, not paying them, or creating a hostile work environment. The employee must feel they have no choice but to leave due to the employer's conduct.

For a claim of constructive dismissal to be valid, the employee usually needs to show that the employer's actions were serious enough to justify leaving and that they resigned promptly after the breach. It is important to follow a fair procedure and consider seeking advice before resigning under these circumstances.

Step 1: Identify the employer's conduct that breaches the contract.  
Step 2: Consider whether the breach is serious enough to justify resignation.  
Step 3: Resign promptly if you decide to claim constructive dismissal.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor before taking further action.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: How long do I need to work somewhere to claim unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

To claim unfair dismissal in the UK, you generally need to have worked for your employer for at least 2 years. This means you must have the legal status of an employee and have been continuously employed for that period. If you meet this requirement and believe your dismissal was unfair, you can make a claim to an employment tribunal. However, there are some exceptions where you can claim unfair dismissal without the 2-year qualifying period, such as if the dismissal was related to discrimination, whistleblowing, or certain other protected reasons.

If you are dismissed, your employer should provide you with the reasons for dismissal in writing if you have been employed for 2 years or more and you request it. They must also follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing you, or you may have grounds for an unfair dismissal claim.

Step 1: Check how long you have been employed and whether you have the legal status of an employee.  
Step 2: If you have worked for 2 years or more, ask your employer for a written explanation of the dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 3: Consider whether the dismissal was fair and if the employer followed a proper procedure.  
Step 4: If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal internally or consider making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer says I was dismissed for poor performance. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says you were dismissed for poor performance, they must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. This means they should have investigated the situation fully and followed a fair procedure before dismissing you. The employer should have given you clear feedback about your performance issues and given you an opportunity to improve. They must also be consistent and not treat you less favourably than other employees in similar situations.

You have the right to be told why you were dismissed, when your contract ends, your notice period, and your right to appeal the decision. If you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012), you can ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which they must provide within 14 days. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal internally and, if necessary, bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Consider whether the employer followed a fair procedure and gave you a chance to improve your performance.  
Step 3: If you think the dismissal was unfair, use your employer’s appeal process.  
Step 4: If the appeal does not resolve the issue, you may consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for further guidance on making a claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be dismissed while on sick leave?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can be dismissed while on sick leave, but your employer must have a fair reason for dismissal and follow a reasonable process. They need to show that they have investigated the situation fully and acted reasonably in the circumstances. For example, if your absence is due to long-term sickness, your employer should consider whether they can make reasonable adjustments or if dismissal is justified. They must also be consistent and not treat you less favourably than other employees in similar situations.

If you are dismissed, your employer must give you the notice period stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer, unless it is a case of gross misconduct. You have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have worked for your employer for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012).

Step 1: Check your employment contract and company policies on sickness absence and dismissal.  
Step 2: If you are dismissed, ask your employer for a written statement of reasons.  
Step 3: If you believe the dismissal was unfair, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What should happen in a fair disciplinary process?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

A fair disciplinary process in the workplace should follow a clear, reasonable, and consistent procedure. The employer must have a valid reason for any disciplinary action and should investigate the matter thoroughly before making a decision. The employee should be informed of the concerns or allegations against them and given an opportunity to respond. The process should also allow the employee to be accompanied by a colleague or trade union representative during disciplinary meetings.

Employers should follow the Acas Code of Practice on disciplinary and grievance procedures, which sets out how to handle disciplinary issues fairly. If a dismissal results from the disciplinary process, the employer must explain the reasons for dismissal, the notice period, and the employee’s right to appeal. The entire procedure should be documented, and confidentiality should be maintained throughout.

Step 1: Employer investigates the issue and gathers evidence.  
Step 2: Employee is informed of the allegations and invited to a disciplinary meeting.  
Step 3: Employee attends the meeting, possibly with a companion, and responds to the allegations.  
Step 4: Employer makes a decision based on the evidence and informs the employee in writing.  
Step 5: Employee is given the right to appeal the decision if they disagree.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be made redundant without consultation?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers are generally required to consult with employees before making them redundant. Consultation is an important part of the redundancy process and should happen before any final decisions are made. This allows employees to discuss the reasons for redundancy, explore alternatives, and raise any concerns. For larger redundancy situations (usually 20 or more employees), there are specific legal requirements for collective consultation.

If you are made redundant without any consultation, this could be unfair dismissal, especially if you have been employed for at least two years. However, the exact circumstances can vary, and it is important to check the details of your situation.

Step 1: Check if your employer has followed a consultation process regarding your redundancy.  
Step 2: If you believe there was no consultation, raise your concerns with your employer or HR department.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor to understand your rights and options.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What is the difference between redundancy and dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal is when an employer ends an employee's contract, which is commonly known as being sacked or fired. It can happen for various reasons, such as misconduct, poor performance, or other valid reasons. The employer must follow a fair and reasonable procedure and have a valid reason to dismiss someone to avoid claims of unfair dismissal.

Redundancy is a specific type of dismissal that occurs when an employer needs to reduce their workforce. This might happen because the business is closing, the job role is no longer needed, or there is less work available. Unlike other dismissals, redundancy is related to the job position rather than the employee's conduct or performance.

In summary, all redundancies are dismissals, but not all dismissals are redundancies. Redundancy is dismissal due to the job no longer existing or being needed.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer dismissed me for raising a complaint. Is this unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer dismissed you because you raised a complaint, this could potentially be considered unfair dismissal, especially if the complaint was made in good faith and related to your working conditions or rights. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and follow a fair and reasonable procedure. Dismissing someone for raising a complaint may be seen as a breach of these requirements. You have the right to appeal the dismissal and your employer should follow a fair process during this appeal.

If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important to act quickly, as there are strict time limits for making such claims. You might also want to seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice to understand your options and the best steps to take.

Step 1: Check if your employer gave you a clear reason for dismissal in writing.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal through your employer’s appeal process.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim for unfair dismissal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I challenge my dismissal at a tribunal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can challenge your dismissal at an employment tribunal if you believe it was unfair. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably in the circumstances, and follow a fair procedure. This includes investigating the situation fully and being consistent in how they treat employees. If you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to request a written statement from your employer explaining the reasons for your dismissal.

If you think your dismissal was unfair, you should first appeal the decision with your employer. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. Keep in mind that if you settle the matter with a settlement agreement, you usually give up the right to make an unfair dismissal claim. It is important to act quickly, as there are strict time limits for making tribunal claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Appeal the dismissal through your employer’s appeal process.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the required time limit (usually 3 months minus one day from the dismissal date).  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice to help with the process.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: What should I do if I believe my dismissal was unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, your employer should have followed a fair and reasonable procedure before dismissing you. This includes having a valid reason for dismissal and making a balanced and consistent decision. You have the right to appeal against the dismissal, and your employer must follow a fair procedure during this appeal. If you successfully appeal and get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," meaning you cannot then claim unfair dismissal at an employment tribunal.

If you want to challenge the dismissal, you should first raise an appeal with your employer. If the appeal does not resolve the issue, you may be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. It is important to act promptly and follow the correct procedures. You can also seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for support.

Step 1: Ask your employer for the reasons for your dismissal in writing if you have not already received them.  
Step 2: Use your employer’s appeal process to challenge the dismissal.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance on making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: I was treated unfairly at work because of my religion. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for your employer or colleagues to treat you unfairly because of your religion or belief. This protection covers all aspects of work, including hiring, pay, promotion, and dismissal. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else's claim.

If you believe you have been discriminated against at work because of your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint. Employers are required to take such complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination and bullying. If the issue is not resolved internally, you may have the right to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document the incidents where you believe you were treated unfairly due to your religion.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If unresolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: My manager is harassing me. Is this discrimination or harassment?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, such as age, sex, race, disability, or religion, which violates your dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. If your manager's behaviour fits this description and is connected to one of these protected characteristics, it may be considered harassment. Harassment is a form of discrimination, and employers have a duty to prevent and address it.

If the behaviour is not related to a protected characteristic but is still unwanted and causes distress, it may be considered bullying rather than discrimination. Employers should take complaints about both harassment and bullying seriously and have procedures to handle them.

Step 1: Document the incidents, including dates, times, what was said or done, and any witnesses.  
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer following their grievance or complaints procedure.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved internally, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your options.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: What is workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 means treating someone unfairly or less favourably because they have a protected characteristic. These protected characteristics include age, gender reassignment, being married or in a civil partnership, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race (including colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin), religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Discrimination can happen directly, such as refusing to hire someone because of their race, or indirectly, such as applying a rule that disadvantages people with a protected characteristic.

The law also protects people who are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or who have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim. Employers have a legal duty to prevent discrimination, take complaints seriously, and handle them properly. Positive action to help people with protected characteristics who are disadvantaged or under-represented is allowed.

Step 1: If you believe you have been discriminated against, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or through a formal complaint procedure.  
Step 2: Your employer should investigate and take appropriate action to stop the discrimination.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about further steps, including employment tribunal claims.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be treated differently because of my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is unlawful for an employer or anyone else to treat you less favourably because of your disability. This means you should not face discrimination at work or in other areas such as education or when using public services. Employers must make reasonable adjustments to accommodate your disability to ensure you are not at a disadvantage compared to others.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly because of your disability, you can raise the issue with your employer or seek advice on how to proceed. Employers are also encouraged to take positive action to support people with disabilities if they face particular challenges or are under-represented.

Step 1: If you experience discrimination, consider raising the issue informally with your employer.
Step 2: If the issue is not resolved, you may make a formal complaint following your employer’s grievance procedure.
Step 3: Seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What is victimisation at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Victimisation at work happens when an employer or colleague treats someone badly because they have made a complaint about discrimination, supported someone else's complaint, or raised a concern about discrimination or bullying. It is a form of unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. Victimisation can include things like being ignored, given less work, or being unfairly disciplined because of these actions.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent victimisation and to take any complaints seriously. If you believe you are being victimised at work, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal complaint procedure.

Step 1: Keep a record of any incidents that you believe are victimisation.  
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer or HR department.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: My employer ignored my complaint about discrimination. What can I do?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has ignored your complaint about discrimination, you have several options. First, you can raise the issue again, either informally or formally, by following your employer’s grievance procedure. Employers are required by law to take discrimination complaints seriously and investigate them properly. If your employer still does not respond or address the issue, you may consider seeking advice from external bodies such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

If the problem remains unresolved, you can make a claim to an employment tribunal for discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. It is important to act promptly, as there are strict time limits for bringing claims. You may also want to keep records of your complaint and any responses or lack thereof from your employer.

Step 1: Raise your complaint again, formally if needed, following your employer’s grievance procedure.  
Step 2: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for support and guidance.  
Step 3: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Is bullying at work illegal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Bullying at work is not a specific criminal offence, but it can be unlawful if it amounts to harassment or discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. Employers have a legal duty to prevent bullying and take complaints seriously. If bullying is related to protected characteristics such as age, race, sex, disability, or other protected categories, it may be considered unlawful discrimination or harassment.

If you experience bullying, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. Employers should have policies and procedures in place to handle bullying and discrimination complaints and take appropriate action to stop it.

Step 1: Document the bullying incidents with dates and details.  
Step 2: Report the bullying to your employer following their grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What counts as harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic that violates a person's dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. Protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010 include age, disability, gender reassignment, race, religion or belief, sex, sexual orientation, pregnancy and maternity, marriage and civil partnership. Harassment can be direct or indirect and may include actions, comments, or conduct that make someone feel uncomfortable or threatened.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent harassment and to take complaints seriously. If you experience harassment, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. Employers should have procedures in place to handle such complaints and take appropriate action to stop the behaviour.

Step 1: Recognise if the behaviour is unwanted and linked to a protected characteristic.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If the employer does not take appropriate action, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice and consider a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Can my employer refuse reasonable adjustments for my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, employers have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments for employees with disabilities to help them overcome disadvantages at work. This means your employer should consider and implement changes that enable you to do your job effectively, such as modifying equipment, adjusting working hours, or altering your work environment. However, what counts as "reasonable" depends on factors like the size and resources of the employer and the practicality of the adjustment.

An employer can refuse to make an adjustment if it would cause them a disproportionate burden or significant difficulty, but they must carefully consider your request and provide a clear explanation if they decide not to proceed. If you believe your employer has unreasonably refused to make adjustments, you can raise the issue informally with them or follow formal grievance procedures. You may also seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Inform your employer about your disability and the adjustments you need.  
Step 2: Discuss possible reasonable adjustments with your employer.  
Step 3: If refused, ask for a clear explanation and consider raising a formal complaint.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if the issue is unresolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: I was treated unfairly because of my race. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal to be treated unfairly or discriminated against because of your race, which includes colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin. This protection applies at work, meaning your employer must not discriminate against you based on your race. You are also protected if you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else's claim.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly because of your race, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or formally, and they are required to take your complaint seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination. If the issue is not resolved internally, you may have the option to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document the incidents of unfair treatment or discrimination.
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, following their grievance procedure if they have one.
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a formal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be dismissed for making a discrimination complaint?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, it is generally unlawful for an employer to dismiss you because you have made a complaint about discrimination. Such a dismissal could be considered automatically unfair, as it may be seen as a form of victimisation under the Equality Act 2010. Employers must not treat employees less favourably for asserting their rights or making a complaint about discrimination.

If you believe you have been dismissed for making a discrimination complaint, you may have grounds to bring a claim for unfair dismissal or discrimination to an employment tribunal. It is important that your employer follows a fair procedure and has a valid reason for dismissal unrelated to your complaint.

Step 1: Keep records of your complaint and any related communications.  
Step 2: Raise your concerns formally with your employer if possible.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor to understand your rights and options.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What should I do if I experience harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you experience harassment at work, you have the right to take action under the Equality Act 2010, which protects you from harassment related to protected characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, and others. Employers are required to take complaints seriously and should have procedures in place to handle harassment and discrimination. You can choose to address the issue informally by speaking to your employer or HR department, or you can make a formal complaint through your employer’s grievance procedure.

It is important to keep a record of incidents, including dates, times, what was said or done, and any witnesses. If your employer does not resolve the issue, you may be able to take your case to an employment tribunal. Employers also have a responsibility to prevent harassment and bullying at work by creating a safe and respectful environment.

Step 1: Document the harassment incidents clearly and factually.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer or HR department, either informally or formally.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps, including the possibility of an employment tribunal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
